channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Building AI Products Isn't Traditional PM Work #ai #productmanagement #engineering
executive_summary: Nate B Jones discusses the evolving role of product managers (PMs) in the age of AI, highlighting that AI introduces unique challenges rather than simply speeding up traditional PM work. He emphasizes the need for PMs to develop technical AI fluency while maintaining their product intuition to navigate this transition successfully.
bullets: Product managers face threats from multiple axes simultaneously, including competition from AI tools and the need for new skills. This is like a chess player who suddenly has to learn new strategies while also facing a stronger opponent — they must adapt quickly or risk losing.

Building AI products is fundamentally different from traditional product work because it requires a deeper understanding of data and algorithms. Think of it like learning to cook with a new ingredient that changes the entire recipe; you can't just follow the old instructions.

Core PM skills that AI cannot replace include empathy, intuition, and contextual understanding of user needs. These skills are like the seasoning in a dish — without them, the meal may be technically correct but will lack flavor.

PMs must ground themselves in technical AI fluency to stay relevant in their roles. This is akin to a pilot needing to understand the mechanics of their aircraft; without that knowledge, they can't effectively navigate the skies.

Outsourcing judgment to AI can lead to poor decision-making, as AI lacks the nuanced understanding of human context. It's like relying solely on a GPS for navigation without knowing how to read a map; you might end up lost in unfamiliar territory.

The transition to AI requires PMs to focus on their product intuition while also embracing new technologies. This balance is like walking a tightrope; if you lean too far in one direction, you risk falling.

AI tools can automate many tasks, but they cannot replace the human touch in product management. Just as a robot can assemble a car but cannot understand the joy of driving it, AI lacks the emotional connection that PMs bring to their products.

The implementation costs of AI tools are expected to drop significantly, potentially going to zero in some cases. This is like finding a way to build a house with no construction costs — it opens up new possibilities for innovation.
PMs need to adapt quickly to the changing landscape of AI, as the pace of change is accelerating. If they don't, they risk becoming obsolete, much like a typewriter in the age of computers.

By the end of the year, PMs should aim to enhance their technical skills and understanding of AI to remain competitive. This is similar to a student needing to pass a crucial exam; without preparation, they may fail to advance.

AI can enhance PM efficiency, but it also raises the bar for what is expected from PMs. It's like a marathon runner who now has to run faster than ever before; the competition is tougher, and only the best will succeed.

The unique AI crisis for PMs is not just about speed but about the depth of understanding required. This is like a musician who must not only play the notes but also understand the theory behind the music to truly excel.
key_takeaways: Develop your technical AI fluency by taking online courses or attending workshops. This is crucial because understanding AI will help you make informed decisions; without this knowledge, you risk falling behind in your career.

Set a rule that at least 50% of your evaluation tests must check for code hygiene, such as naming conventions and structure. Most teams currently focus too much on functionality, which can lead to messy code that is hard to maintain.
By the end of the year, prioritize learning about AI tools and their applications in product management. If you don't, you may find yourself outpaced by colleagues who have embraced these changes.

technical_terms.AI Fluency: The ability to understand and effectively communicate about artificial intelligence technologies and their implications. Think of it like learning a new language; without fluency, you can't fully engage in conversations.
technical_terms.Product Intuition: The innate ability to understand user needs and market trends, guiding product development decisions. Like a chef who knows how to balance flavors without a recipe, it's about instinctively knowing what works.
technical_terms.Code Hygiene: The practice of writing clean, maintainable code that follows best practices in naming, structure, and function length. Think of it like keeping your kitchen clean; a tidy space makes cooking easier and more enjoyable.
technical_terms.Implementation Costs: The expenses associated with deploying a new technology or system. It's like the upfront cost of buying a car; you need to consider not just the purchase price but also maintenance and fuel.
technical_terms.Automation: The use of technology to perform tasks with minimal human intervention. Like a washing machine that cleans clothes without you needing to scrub them by hand, automation saves time and effort.
technical_terms.User Needs: The requirements and desires of the end-users of a product. Think of it like a tailor who must understand the client's preferences to create a perfect-fitting suit.
technical_terms.Empathy: The ability to understand and share the feelings of others, crucial for product managers to design user-centered products. Like a friend who listens and understands your problems, empathy helps create better solutions.
technical_terms.Contextual Understanding: The awareness of the environment and circumstances surrounding a user's interaction with a product. It's like knowing the backstory of a character in a movie; it adds depth to the viewing experience.
technical_terms.Data Algorithms: Step-by-step procedures or formulas for solving problems using data. Think of it like a recipe that tells you exactly how to combine ingredients to achieve a delicious dish.
technical_terms.Competitive Landscape: The overall environment in which businesses compete, including their strengths and weaknesses. It's like a sports league where teams must understand their opponents to strategize effectively.
